applied_design_conclusions: Canonical writer must create temp file in destination directory to avoid cross-device rename problems.
Canonical writer must flush and fsync temp file before os.replace, then fsync parent directory where available.
Canonical writer must canonicalize destination and enforce allowlisted roots before opening temp file.
Canonical writer must reject symlink targets and symlink ancestors unless an explicit later policy allows otherwise.
Canonical writer must validate JSON serialization before touching the target path.
research_method: web.run plus direct runtime artifact inspection

sources.claim: Same-directory temporary file followed by atomic move/replace is the normal atomic-write pattern; atomicwrites uses a temp file in the same directory so it is on the same filesystem, then moves it atomically.
The atomicwrites package documents POSIX fsync on the temp file and fsync on the parent directory after move to flush the filename/directory entry.
Python os.replace provides overwrite replacement semantics appropriate for cross-platform destination replacement in Python 3.3+.
OWASP defines path traversal as filesystem access outside the intended directory through ../ or absolute-path manipulation; canonical path validation and root allowlisting are required controls.
Android security guidance describes path traversal as attacker-controlled filesystem paths without validation and recommends validation before filesystem APIs.
